stem: In gradient descent, the gradient tells the model:

options.text: The exact parameters to use for minimum loss
The direction in parameter space where the loss decreases most steeply
How many training examples to use per step
The optimal learning rate

explanation_after: The gradient points in the direction of steepest *ascent* in the loss landscape. By moving in the *negative* gradient direction, we step toward lower loss — hence 'gradient descent'.

stem: What is the role of the loss function in machine learning?

options.text: To randomise the initial weights of the model
To measure how wrong the model's predictions are — the signal the model learns from
To control how many layers the neural network has
To split the data into train and test sets

explanation_after: The loss function quantifies the error between predictions and ground truth. Training minimises this function by adjusting weights. Without a loss function, there is no learning signal.

stem: If the learning rate is set too high, what typically happens?

options.text: Training is too slow
The model perfectly memorises the training data
The model's parameters oscillate or diverge, and training fails to converge
The model always underfits

explanation_after: A high learning rate causes the optimizer to overshoot the minimum, bouncing around or diverging. Loss may increase erratically instead of decreasing steadily.

options.text: Generates new training data
Initialises neural network weights randomly
Computes the gradient of the loss with respect to each weight using the chain rule
Selects which features to include in the model

explanation_after: Backpropagation applies the chain rule to efficiently compute gradients through each layer of the network, from the output loss backward to every weight. These gradients are then used by the optimizer.

stem: What is the purpose of an epoch in training?

options.text: A single weight update step
One complete pass through the entire training dataset
The number of neurons in one layer
The point at which training stops

explanation_after: An epoch is one complete pass through the training data. Models are typically trained for multiple epochs, seeing each example many times as weights are gradually refined.

stem: Why does mini-batch gradient descent often outperform both batch and stochastic gradient descent?

options.text: It uses less memory than any other method
It always finds the global minimum
It balances computational efficiency with a useful regularising effect from gradient noise
It requires no learning rate

explanation_after: Mini-batch GD is computationally efficient (uses GPU parallelism) and the noise from each mini-batch helps escape shallow local minima. Pure batch GD is slow; pure SGD (1 sample) is too noisy.
